sentences.base_language: Seongjun had been meaning to get back into shape for months and finally signed up for a gym membership.
sentences.target_language: 성준은 몇 달째 다시 몸을 만들어야겠다고 생각해 오다가, 마침내 헬스장 회원권을 끊었다.
sentences.romanized: Seongjun-eun myeot daljjae dasi mom-eul mandeureoagessda-go saenggakae oda-ga, maminae helseujang hoewongwon-eul kkeunheotda.
literal: that [he] really must get back into shape (quoted resolution)
had been thinking (ongoing up to now)-and then,
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어 오다** ongoing action up to now: 생각해 **오다가** (had been thinking all along — and then). **-아/어 오다** marks an action sustained from the past up to the present: 노력해 **왔다** (has been making efforts), 기다려 **왔다** (has been waiting). Combined with **몇 달째** (for months now) it emphasises the prolonged, unresolved intention before the decisive moment.
**-다가** interruption / shift of action: 생각해 오**다가** 끊었다 (had been thinking about it — and then finally signed up). **-다가** attaches to a verb to show that an action was in progress when something changed: 가**다가** 돌아왔다 (was going and then turned back). Here it captures the precise moment the long-held intention gave way to concrete action.

sentences.base_language: On his first morning, he arrived early and felt a mix of motivation and slight intimidation.
sentences.target_language: 첫날 아침, 그는 일찍 도착했고 의욕과 약간의 위축감이 뒤섞인 기분을 느꼈다.
sentences.romanized: Cheotnal achim, geu-neun illijjik dochakaetgo uiyok-gwa yakgan-eui wichukgam-i dwiseokin gibun-eul neukkkeotda.
literal: motivation and a slight sense of intimidation-SUBJECT
sentences.grammarNote:
**뒤섞이다** = to be mixed together, to be intermingled: **뒤섞인** 기분 (a feeling in which [emotions] were mixed together). **뒤-** is an intensifying prefix suggesting disorder or thoroughness, and **섞이다** is the passive of **섞다** (to mix): 감정이 **뒤섞였다** (emotions were all mixed up). As a past modifier **뒤섞인** it describes a compound emotional state concisely.
**-(으)ㄴ 기분을 느끼다** = to feel a [described] feeling: **뒤섞인 기분을 느꼈다** (felt a mixed feeling). This construction nominalises the quality of the feeling with a modifier and makes it the object of 느끼다 (to feel): 설레는 **기분을 느꼈다** (felt an excited feeling), 낯선 **기분을 느꼈다** (felt an unfamiliar feeling). It is a natural way to capture blended or complex emotional states.

sentences.base_language: A friendly personal trainer at the front desk offered to show him around the equipment.
sentences.target_language: 안내 데스크에 있던 친절한 트레이너 한 명이 기구 사용법을 안내해 주겠다고 했다.
sentences.romanized: Annae desukeu-e itdeon chinjeolhan teureinео han myeong-i gigu sayongbeop-eul annaehae jugessda-go haetda.
literal: who was at the front desk (past modifier)
the equipment usage / how to use the equipment-OBJ
that [he/she] would show around / guide (quoted benefactive intention)
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어 주다** benefactive: **안내해 주겠다고** (said [he/she] would show [him] around — as a favour). **-아/어 주다** marks an action performed for another person's benefit: 가르쳐 **주다** (teach for someone), 도와 **주다** (help someone). The **-겠-** inside the quoted clause expresses the trainer's willing intention, and **-고 하다** reports the speech indirectly: 안내해 주**겠다고 했다** (said [they] would be glad to guide).
**-고 있던** past progressive modifier (in background): 안내 데스크에 **있던** 트레이너 (the trainer who was at the front desk). **있다** (to be/exist) + **-던** (retrospective modifier): **있던** here describes a person who was stationed at a location at the relevant past moment. **-던** as a retrospective modifier suits background-scene descriptions: 창가에 **앉아 있던** 사람 (the person who was sitting by the window).

sentences.base_language: The trainer explained that consistency was more important than intensity, especially at the start.
sentences.target_language: 트레이너는 특히 처음에는 강도보다 꾸준함이 더 중요하다고 설명해 주었다.
sentences.romanized: Teureinео-neun teuki cheoeumeneun gangdo-boda kkujunham-i deo jungyohada-go seolmyeonghae jueotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-보다** comparative particle: **강도보다** 꾸준함이 더 중요하다 (consistency is more important than intensity). **-보다** (than) marks the standard of comparison, always preceding **더** (more): 어제**보다** 오늘이 더 춥다 (today is colder than yesterday). It is the single most productive comparative structure in Korean and requires no additional copula.
**꾸준함** nominalised adjective: **꾸준하다** (to be consistent / steady) + **-ㅁ** (nominaliser) → **꾸준함** (consistency / steadiness). The suffix **-ㅁ/-음** turns an adjective or verb into an abstract noun: 아름다움 (beauty), 고마움 (gratitude). Using the nominalised form as the grammatical subject gives it the same weight as a content noun like 강도 (intensity), enabling a direct, balanced comparison.

sentences.base_language: Seongjun started with twenty minutes on the treadmill, which felt manageable at first.
sentences.target_language: 성준은 처음에 러닝머신으로 이십 분을 시작했는데, 처음에는 해볼 만하다는 느낌이었다.
sentences.romanized: Seongjun-eun cheoeum-e reoning meosin-euro isip bun-eul sijakhaenneunde, cheoeumeneun haebол manhadaneun neukkimeotda.
literal: that [it] was manageable / worth trying (quoted modifier)
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄹ 만하다** worthwhile / manageable: **해볼 만하다** (worth trying / manageable). **-(으)ㄹ 만하다** expresses that something is sufficiently doable or worthwhile: 볼 **만하다** (worth watching), 먹을 **만하다** (worth eating). Here 해볼 **만하다** = 'worth giving a go / not beyond one's ability' — an honest admission of cautious optimism rather than confidence.
**-는데** background / contrast connector: 시작했**는데** 해볼 만하다는 느낌이었다 (started — and [at first] it felt manageable). **-는데** sets up a background situation or soft contrast, allowing the second clause to elaborate or pivot: 갔**는데** 문이 닫혀 있었다 (went — and the door was closed). Its use here signals that the 'manageable' feeling is qualified information that will soon be complicated.

sentences.base_language: By the tenth minute, his legs were heavy, his lungs were burning, and sweat was dripping down his face.
sentences.target_language: 십 분이 지나자 다리는 무거워지고, 폐는 타는 듯했으며, 얼굴에는 땀이 흘러내리고 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Sip bun-i jinaja dari-neun muegeowojigo, pye-neun taneun deuthaetseumyeo, eolgul-eneun ttam-i heulleonaerigo isseotda.
literal: his lungs seemed as if burning-and (formal),
sentences.grammarNote:
**-자** immediacy: 십 분이 지나**자** 다리가 무거워졌다 (the moment ten minutes had passed, his legs became heavy). **-자** expresses immediate temporal succession — one event triggering the next without delay: 문을 열**자** 찬 바람이 들어왔다 (the moment [he] opened the door, cold air came in). It is more sudden and tightly linked than **-고 나서** (after doing).
**-아/어지다** change of state: **무거워지고** (became heavy — change in progress). **무겁다** (heavy) + **-아/어지다** → **무거워지다** (to become heavy). **-아/어지다** converts an adjective into a dynamic change-of-state verb: 어려워**지다** (become difficult), 따뜻해**지다** (become warm). The **-고** connector lists three simultaneous physical changes, building a compounding sense of fatigue.

sentences.base_language: He slowed the machine to a walking pace, telling himself this still counted as progress.
sentences.target_language: 그는 기계를 걷는 속도로 늦추면서, 이것도 여전히 발전이라고 스스로에게 타일렀다.
sentences.romanized: Geu-neun gigye-reul geodneun sokdo-ro neutchumyeonseo, igeotdo yeojeonhi baljeon-irago seuseuro-ege tailleotda.
literal: while slowing [it] to a walking pace-while,
sentences.grammarNote:
**-면서** simultaneous actions: 늦추**면서** 타일렀다 (while slowing [it] down, [he] told himself). **-면서** indicates two concurrent actions by the same subject: 음악을 들으**면서** 공부했다 (studied while listening to music). Here the physical adjustment (slowing the machine) and the internal self-persuasion happen at the same time, capturing a relatable gym moment with precision.
**타이르다** = to talk someone into, to reason with, to tell gently but firmly: **스스로에게 타일렀다** (told himself / reasoned with himself). **타이르다** implies a gentle but earnest verbal persuasion, often from a position of care: 아이를 **타이르다** (reason with a child). Using it reflexively — **스스로에게** — gives the self-talk a slightly tender, almost parental quality: he was gently coaching himself rather than just thinking.

sentences.base_language: Next, the trainer showed him a series of strength exercises using dumbbells.
sentences.target_language: 다음으로 트레이너는 덤벨을 이용한 일련의 근력 운동을 알려 주었다.
sentences.romanized: Daeumeuro teureinео-neun deombel-eul iyonghan illyeon-eui geunlyeok undong-eul allyeo jueotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어 주다** benefactive: **알려 주었다** (showed [him] / let [him] know — as a service). **알리다** (to inform/show) + **-아/어 주다**: the trainer performed the action for Seongjun's benefit: 가르쳐 **주다** (teach for someone), 열어 **주다** (open for someone). In instructional or service contexts, **-아/어 주다** is near-obligatory — omitting it would make the trainer sound indifferent.
**이용한** past modifier of **이용하다**: **덤벨을 이용한** 운동 (exercises that use dumbbells). **이용하다** (to use / to make use of) + **-(으)ㄴ** (past modifier) → **이용한**: the modifier describes the type of exercise by the equipment it utilises. In Korean, the modifying clause precedes the noun it modifies, so **덤벨을 이용한** modifies **운동** (exercise): 기구를 **이용한** 훈련 (training using equipment).

sentences.base_language: Seongjun's arms trembled on the last two repetitions of each set, which the trainer said was a good sign.
sentences.target_language: 세트마다 마지막 두 개의 반복 동작에서 성준의 팔이 떨렸는데, 트레이너는 그것이 좋은 신호라고 했다.
sentences.romanized: Seteuмаda majimak du gae-eui banbок dongjak-eseo Seongjun-eui pal-i tteollyeonneunde, teureinео-neun geugeosi joeun sinho-rago haetda.
literal: that that was a good sign (quoted content)
sentences.grammarNote:
**-마다** distributive particle: **세트마다** (every set / each and every set). **-마다** attached to a noun means 'every [noun] / each [noun]': 날**마다** (every day), 사람**마다** (each person). It distributes the event across all instances uniformly, reinforcing that the trembling was consistent and therefore meaningful — not an anomaly.
**-는데** background with reported speech: 떨렸**는데** 트레이너는 좋은 신호라고 했다 (the arms trembled — and [to this] the trainer said it was a good sign). **-는데** here sets up the observed fact as a background from which the trainer's comment emerges as a response. It is a very natural connector for presenting a situation followed by someone's reaction or evaluation of it.

sentences.base_language: After the session, every muscle in his body ached in a deep but not unpleasant way.
sentences.target_language: 운동이 끝난 후, 온몸의 근육 하나하나가 깊지만 불쾌하지는 않은 방식으로 욱신거렸다.
sentences.romanized: Undong-i kkeunnan hu, onmom-eui geunyuk hanahana-ga gipjiman bulkwaehajiineun aneun bangsik-euro uksingeollyeotda.
literal: every muscle in his whole body
sentences.grammarNote:
**하나하나** distributive reduplication: 근육 **하나하나가** (every single muscle / each muscle one by one). **하나** (one) reduplicated → **하나하나** (each and every one, one by one). Korean reduplication of numerals or counters creates a distributive or exhaustive reading: 하나**하나** 확인하다 (check one by one). It is far more emphatic than simply **모든** (all/every).
**욱신거리다** = to throb, to ache with a pulsing sensation: **욱신거렸다** (throbbed / ached rhythmically). **욱신** is a mimetic (의태어) root evoking a dull, repetitive aching pulse, and **-거리다** (to keep doing) makes it an ongoing, iterative sensation: 상처가 **욱신거린다** (the wound keeps throbbing). Using a mimetic verb here is much more physically evocative than plain **아프다** (to hurt).

sentences.base_language: He sat in the locker room for a few minutes, too tired to move but feeling genuinely proud.
sentences.target_language: 그는 탈의실에 몇 분 동안 앉아 있었다. 움직이기엔 너무 피곤했지만, 진심으로 뿌듯함을 느끼고 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Geu-neun taruisil-e myeot bun dongan anja isseotda. Umjigigi-en neomu pigonhaetjiman, jinsim-euro ppudeutham-eul neukkigo isseotda.
literal: sat for a few minutes (resultant posture).
as for moving / in terms of moving
sentences.grammarNote:
**-기엔** = **-기에는** contraction, 'as for [doing]': **움직이기엔** 너무 피곤했다 (was too tired [as for] moving / too tired to move). **-기에** marks the action as the reference point for the following evaluation, often with **너무** (too): 먹**기엔** 너무 뜨겁다 (too hot to eat). This construction is more colloquially compact than the full **-기에는** and very natural in spoken and written Korean.
**뿌듯하다** = to feel a warm sense of pride and fulfillment: **뿌듯함을 느끼고 있었다** (was feeling a sense of pride/fulfillment). **뿌듯하다** is a uniquely Korean emotional descriptor conveying a swelling, wholesome satisfaction — the feeling after doing something hard and worthwhile. The nominalized form **뿌듯함** + **느끼다** (to feel) as object makes the emotion concrete and tangible rather than a mere state.

sentences.base_language: He drank a large bottle of water and ate a banana on a bench outside.
sentences.target_language: 그는 밖의 벤치에 앉아 큰 물 한 병을 다 마시고 바나나도 먹었다.
sentences.romanized: Geu-neun bak-eui benchi-e anja keun mul han byeong-eul da masigo banana-do meogeotda.
literal: 큰 물 한 병을 다 마시고
drank a large bottle of water entirely-and
sentences.grammarNote:
**다** adverb of completion: 물 한 병을 **다** 마시다 (drink a whole bottle of water / finish the entire bottle). **다** (all / entirely) placed before a verb marks the complete consumption or completion of the object: 숙제를 **다** 했다 (finished all the homework), 밥을 **다** 먹었다 (ate all the rice). It is one of the simplest and most frequent completive adverbs in Korean.
**-고** sequential connector for a simple list of actions: 마시**고** 먹었다 (drank and then ate). **-고** links two sequential or simultaneous actions of the same subject without implying cause or contrast: 씻**고** 잤다 (washed and then slept). Used here for the quiet, restorative routine after exercise — the simplicity of the grammar matches the simplicity of the moment.

sentences.base_language: Walking home, his legs felt heavy but his mood had lifted enormously.
sentences.target_language: 집으로 걸어가면서 다리는 무거웠지만, 기분은 몰라보게 좋아져 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Jib-euro georeogamyeonseo dari-neun mugeowosjiman, gibun-eun mollaboge joajyeo isseotda.
literal: had become good / had lifted (resultant state).
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어지다** change-of-state: **좋아져 있었다** (had become good / had lifted — and remained so). **좋다** (good) + **-아/어지다** → **좋아지다** (to become good/improve). The resultant-state form **-아/어져 있다** emphasises that the improvement had occurred and its effect was still present: 달라져 **있었다** (had changed and stayed changed). This is subtly different from a simple past **좋아졌다** (became good).
**몰라보게** = beyond recognition, dramatically: **몰라보게** 좋아지다 (improved beyond recognition / lifted enormously). **모르다** (not to know / not to recognise) + **-아/어 보다** → idiom **몰라보다** (not to recognise) + **-게** (adverbialiser): literally 'to a degree that one wouldn't recognise [it]'. It is a vivid Korean idiom for dramatic change: **몰라보게** 변했다 (changed beyond recognition), **몰라보게** 성장했다 (grew tremendously).

sentences.base_language: That evening, his muscles began to stiffen and he had to sit down very carefully.
sentences.target_language: 그날 저녁, 근육이 굳어 오기 시작하더니 매우 조심스럽게 앉아야 했다.
sentences.romanized: Geunal jeonyeok, geunyuk-i gudeo ogi sijakhadeoeni maeu josimseureobge anja-ya haetda.
literal: began to come to stiffen — and then (evidential progression)
sentences.grammarNote:
**-기 시작하다** onset of an action: 굳어 오**기 시작했다** (began to stiffen). **-기 시작하다** marks the beginning of an action or process: 비가 오**기 시작했다** (it began to rain). Here it is embedded with **-아/어 오다** (gradual approach): 굳어 **오다** (to gradually stiffen, approaching the present) + **-기 시작하다** = the stiffening process began and started moving in — a creeping, organic onset.
**-더니** retrospective discovery leading to consequence: 굳어 오기 시작하**더니** 앉아야 했다 (the muscles started to stiffen — and as a result [he] had to sit down carefully). **-더니** reports a past-observed situation that led directly to an outcome: 열심히 공부하**더니** 합격했다 (studied hard — and sure enough passed). It gives the sentence a slightly narrative, retrospective flavour — as if recounting the progression of events.

sentences.base_language: Despite the soreness, Seongjun felt a sense of determination he hadn't felt in years.
sentences.target_language: 근육통에도 불구하고, 성준은 수년 만에 느껴 보지 못했던 단단한 의지를 느꼈다.
sentences.romanized: Geunyuktong-edo bulguhago, Seongjun-eun sunyeon man-e neukyeo boji mothaetdeon dandanhan uiji-reul neukkkeotda.
literal: for the first time in years
had not been able to experience / had not felt (past retrospective modifier)
sentences.grammarNote:
**-에도 불구하고** concessive: **근육통에도 불구하고** (despite the muscle soreness). **-에도 불구하고** is the formal concessive construction meaning 'in spite of / regardless of': 비에도 **불구하고** 갔다 (went despite the rain). It is slightly more formal than **-지만** (but) and suits the narrative's reflective, elevated register at an emotional turning point.
**-(으)ㄴ 만에** = for the first time in [period]: **수년 만에** (for the first time in years). **만에** follows a time expression to mean 'after [period] / for the first time in [period]': 오랜만에 (after a long time), 일 년 **만에** (for the first time in a year). Here it underscores how long Seongjun has been disconnected from this feeling of determination — making its return especially meaningful.

sentences.base_language: He downloaded a fitness app and set a goal of going to the gym three times a week.
sentences.target_language: 그는 피트니스 앱을 다운로드하고, 일주일에 세 번 헬스장에 가기로 목표를 세웠다.
sentences.romanized: Geu-neun piteuneseu aep-eul daunlodeu hago, iljuil-e se beon helseujang-e gagi-ro mokpyo-reul sae-wotda.
literal: to go to the gym (nominalised decision)
sentences.grammarNote:
**-기로 하다** decision: **가기로** 목표를 세웠다 (set a goal of going). **-기로** (nominaliser + direction particle 로) creates a noun clause that functions as the content of a decision or plan: **가기로 했다** (decided to go), **공부하기로** 목표를 세웠다 (set a goal of studying). Here it is the complement of **목표를 세우다** (to set a goal), linking the concrete resolution (going three times a week) to the abstract act of goal-setting.
**목표를 세우다** = to set a goal: **목표를 세웠다** (set a goal). **목표** (goal/objective) + **세우다** (to erect / to establish): **세우다** is the standard collocate for creating plans and goals in Korean: 계획을 **세우다** (make a plan), 전략을 **세우다** (establish a strategy). It implies deliberate, structured intention — more committed than simply 생각하다 (to think about).

sentences.base_language: The session two days later was noticeably easier — his body had already begun to adapt.
sentences.target_language: 이틀 후의 운동은 눈에 띄게 더 수월했다. 몸이 벌써 적응하기 시작하고 있었던 것이다.
sentences.romanized: Iteul hu-eui undong-eun nun-e ttwige deo suwolhaetda. Mom-i beolsseo jeogeunghagi sijakago isseotdeon geosida.
literal: was in the process of having begun to adapt (explanatory retrospective).
sentences.grammarNote:
**-기 시작하다** onset: 적응하**기 시작하고 있었던** 것이다 (it was that [his body] had begun to adapt). **적응하기 시작하다** (to begin to adapt) in the past progressive retrospective form **-고 있었던** (was in the state of doing): the adaptation was already underway — it had begun and was ongoing. The layering of **-기 시작하다** + **-고 있었던** captures a process that had both started and was continuing.
**-(으)ㄴ 것이다** explanatory ending: **적응하기 시작하고 있었던 것이다** (it was that [his body] had already begun to adapt). **-것이다** after a past modifier transforms the statement into an explanation or revelation of the underlying reason: 그래서 그랬던 **것이다** (so that was why). It gives the sentence the feel of a conclusion drawn from evidence — the easier session is explained by the body's rapid adaptation.

sentences.base_language: The trainer greeted him by name and said his form had already improved considerably.
sentences.target_language: 트레이너는 그를 이름으로 반기면서, 자세가 이미 상당히 좋아졌다고 말해 주었다.
sentences.romanized: Teureinео-neun geu-reul ireum-euro bangimyeonseo, jase-ga imi sangdanghi joajyeotdago malhae jueotda.
literal: that his form had already improved considerably (quoted content)
sentences.grammarNote:
**-면서** simultaneous actions: **반기면서** 말해 주었다 (greeted [him] by name while telling [him]). **반기다** (to welcome / to greet gladly) + **-면서**: the greeting and the compliment happened at the same time — a warm, combined gesture. **-면서** is essential here because the form of address and the content of encouragement are inseparable parts of one welcoming moment.
**반기다** = to greet warmly, to welcome with pleasure: **이름으로 반기면서** (greeting him by name / welcoming him by name). **반기다** implies delight at someone's arrival — more affectionate than the neutral **인사하다** (to greet): 오랜 친구를 **반기다** (warmly welcome an old friend). Being greeted by name signals recognition and belonging — a small but meaningful milestone in Seongjun's gym journey.

sentences.base_language: After a month of regular training, his energy and mood had changed in ways people around him started to notice.
sentences.target_language: 한 달간의 꾸준한 훈련 끝에, 그의 에너지와 기분이 주변 사람들도 알아챌 정도로 달라져 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Han dalgan-eui kkujunhan hunlyeon kkeut-e, geu-eui eneoji-wa gibun-i jubyeon saramdeul-do alаchal jeongdo-ro dallajyeo isseotda.
literal: at the end of a month of consistent training,
to the degree that even the people around him could notice
had become different / had changed (resultant state).
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄹ 정도로** extent / degree: 알아챌 **정도로** 달라졌다 (changed to the degree that [people] could notice). **-(으)ㄹ 정도로** expresses the extent of a change or quality: 믿기 어려울 **정도로** 빨랐다 (was fast to an almost unbelievable degree). Combined with **주변 사람들도** (even the people around him) it emphasises how visible and significant the transformation had become — not just felt internally but observable externally.
**달라지다** = to become different, to change: **달라져 있었다** (had become different — and remained so). **다르다** (to be different) + **-아/어지다** → **달라지다**: a change-of-state verb. The resultant form **달라져 있었다** stresses that the change was complete and stable — not a transient fluctuation but a settled new reality. This pairs with **-끝에** (at the end of / after the culmination of) to mark the achievement as earned through sustained effort.

sentences.base_language: What had started as an obligation had quietly become something he genuinely looked forward to each week.
sentences.target_language: 의무감으로 시작했던 것이, 어느새 매주 진심으로 기대하게 되는 무언가가 되어 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Uimugam-euro sijakhaetdeon geosi, eonusae maeju jinsim-euro gidaehage doeineun mueonga-ga doeo isseotda.
literal: the thing that had started as a sense of obligation-SUBJECT,
to the point of genuinely looking forward to each week
sentences.grammarNote:
**-게 되다** coming to be / natural transition: **기대하게 되다** (come to look forward to / end up looking forward to). **-게 되다** expresses a change of state or situation that came about naturally, without deliberate effort: 좋아하**게 됐다** (came to like), 알**게 됐다** (came to know). Here it captures the organic, unforced nature of the shift — he didn't force himself to enjoy the gym; it happened on its own.
**어느새** = before one knows it, imperceptibly, quietly: **어느새** 기대하게 됐다 (quietly came to look forward to it / before he knew it). **어느새** (from **어느** + **사이에**: 'at some point between') describes a change that happened so gradually it went unnoticed: **어느새** 밤이 됐다 (before [we] knew it, night had fallen). It is the perfect adverb for the story's closing image of slow, quiet transformation.
